data.title: Coda Octopus: A Risky Bet On Future Growth From Military Applications
data.description:
Introduction

Coda Octopus Group, Inc. (CODA) caught my attention because of the decent price correction it experienced recently, and now that it has had its Q1 results recently published, I thought it would be a good time to see if it would be a good time to invest. I feel like with small-cap stocks like CODA, there is a lot of risk involved, but from time to time, I like to take some risks and invest in smaller companies, as the growth potential is a lot more enticing. Therefore, I will be starting a position shortly, as I like the product and the outlook and the fact that it gives me more diversification away from the AI craze.

Briefly on the Company

CODA is a small underwater tech company that sells and rents high-end sonar and diving systems that aid the diver to see and work underwater in zero visibility. Most of the money it makes is from its top-of-the-line real-time 3D imaging sonar product, called Echoscope, and the next big driver in the future, DAVD. AR diver helmet system, used in subsea construction as well as many subsea defense applications.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, revenue came in at $6.7m, up around 29% y/y, which is respectable. Looking at the breakdown in sales in more detail, we can see that the Marine Technology Business, its core sonar, brought it $3.4m of the total revenues and grew 47.4% y/y from $2.3m. MTB represents around 50% of the company’s total revenue. The defense engineering services brought in around $1.8m, up a little over 9% y/y, while its Acoustic Sensors & Materials business grew almost 21% y/y to bring in the rest of the revenues of $1.6m.

Let’s get a little more granular. Within Marine Tech, equipment sales brought in around $2.27m in sales, translating to 31% growth y/y, while rental revenue brought in around $750K, which may not look like a lot, but it’s around 233% growth y/y from $220K last year. Echoscope and DAVD helped the equipment sales’ performance, while the European rental hub drove the rental hub performance.

It is worth noting that Echoscope is said to account for around 80% of Marine Tech revenue, with its sales to Asia growing a whopping 63.4% to $2.6m, so the majority of sales come from Asia.

Let’s take a look at the profitability and efficiency of CODA operations. Overall margins decreased by around 70 bps to 65.1%. This is attributed to a small change in mix and geography, but particularly due to the Defense Engineering Service business performance, as well as the consolidated commission costs increase. Operating margin, on the other hand, increased by around 240 bps due to sales growth exceeding expense growth, which helps increase the company’s cost leverage.

Now digging deeper into the margin profiles of each segment, the “Products” [MTB] saw its gross margins increase from 73.1% to 75.3%, or 220 bps. The Acoustic Sensors and Materials business gross margin was 66.8%, which showed an increase of 510 bps. That is quite an improvement. The Defense Engineering Services gross margin decreased by quite a bit, by 1,480 bps to be exact, and no, that is not a typo. The segment’s efficiency decreased by almost 15%. This drop in efficiency can be attributed to the US funding delays that slowed the awarding of contracts. So, what this essentially means is that its product mix slightly shifted this past quarter. A higher share of work must have come from lower-margin contracts, as opposed to the very lucrative federal ones. I see this as a temporary problem, and I don’t doubt we will see the margins improving once again.

Going over the company’s financial position this past quarter, CODA finished Q1 ’26 with around $30.4m in cash and equivalents against essentially no debt on its books, apart from some operating lease liabilities. The company’s cash position is exceptional. CODA is a small-cap company. Cash alone represents 22% of the company’s valuation. The company is in a very strong position and will no doubt continue to operate and improve further. Additionally, cash from operations more than doubled to $1.64m, and free cash flow improved to $1.39m from $513K. That’s a really good result for a company of this size.

Overall, I would say the company is growing at a very decent and sustainable pace. The results showed good growth across the board, and even the decrease in margins in DES, I see it as a fluke that will sort itself out over the next few quarters. CODA’s balance sheet is very strong, and its cash generation is also noteworthy.

Comments on the Outlook

Let’s dig a little deeper into the company’s premier product offerings to get a better look at what is going to drive CODA’s success in the future. Starting with Echoscope.

Echoscope

As the name implies, Echoscope generates thousands of acoustic beams per ping and renders a live, geo-referenced 3D map, or an acoustic video, like echolocation of whatever is happening deep underwater. It doesn’t need light as it can map even in zero-visibility waters. There are a lot of uses for this product, including port and harbor security, subsea construction, as well as cable/pipeline installation and inspection/repair/maintenance, or IRM for short. What is also very useful for that can save lives is the clearing of old unexploded ordnance or munitions that didn’t detonate properly and may contain live explosives. The product allows to locate these weapons, to identify what they are, and then either removing them or destroying them in a safe manner. This product is very easy to operate and can be mounted on pretty much any sort of ship imaginable. It’s very versatile, which is the big reason for its success.

The management was very positive on the next-gen series of Echoscope, the NanoGen series, which is designed to be much smaller but just as powerful, if not more, to lead the operational performance improvements in the future. This model is also specifically designed to be mounted on autonomous and unmanned subsea vehicles and diver wearables. In the latest opening remarks of the quarter, the management said that they see the UUV market expansion going from around $4.8B to $11B by 2030. How much of the expansion CODA is going to capture here remains to be seen, but these numbers look promising. We won’t have to wait too long to see now, since the management expects to see initial adoption under defense-funded PIPs starting in Q3-Q4 of this year. I’m pretty sure it is going to be a tailwind.

Diver Augmented Vision Display

Or, as it is known as DAVD, it is the company’s next big bet on growth, calling it a disruptive diver technology, which is an end-to-end diver management system that provides a complete topside-plus-diver system with a see-through head-up display that is integrated into the diver’s helmet. This essentially means the diver and the operator up top see the same image. In the image below, you will see an example of what the diver will see in zero-visibility water conditions. This is achievable with the help of Echoscope, overlaying 3D sonar imagery, showing waypoints, models, maps, video, and other sensor data in the diver’s helmet. All these features help with improving situational awareness dramatically. This is particularly important for extremely risky operations, whether military or otherwise. I can see how this could be a popular product among the federal clients.

Currently, the untethered DAVD variant is undergoing Navy safety qualifications and authorization, with the expectation to have this resolved in the next quarter, which will help DAVD’s revenues accelerate and improve margins overall. The management said that there are multiple defense programs already very interested in having DAVD integrated as a core life-support and visualization component, so I am expecting quite good things in the future going forward.

The good thing about DAVD is that it is not dependent solely on the U.S. for revenue generation. It has qualifications in the U.S. and EU, so the market reach is immense, but I’m sure the US will be the leader here once everything comes through.

The application of these products doesn’t rely on governmental defense programs, but I am going to bet that we will see a lot of growth over the next year or so, specifically from these defense programs, not just in the U.S. but globally.

Many countries are on edge right now due to all of the geopolitical risks and escalations of warfare across many different regions, like Iran and Ukraine, which will most likely lead to many governments increasing their military defense spending over the next few years, and that means upgrading naval and underwater infrastructure to be able to protect their respective regions from harm. Recent contracts like the U.S. and foreign navy orders show that defense customers are indeed actively upgrading their capabilities due to more troublesome waters that might still be ahead.

Risks and Closing Comments

There may be further delays in the U.S. defense budgets that delay awards further, creating lumpiness in the operations of the company. This can be seen as a negative because the business is not improving.

DAVD specifically is partially time-dependent on U.S. Navy approvals and procurement cycles. The management still expects everything to go smoothly with the ramp-up, but that can always change in just one quarter if there are further delays.

Further delays may continue to push the company’s margin profile, specifically in the DES, further down, which is obviously a negative.

Concentration of revenues is also a risk. As we found out earlier, around 80% of Marine Tech revenues are from echoscopes, so if there is any slowdown in sales due to competition or a tech disruption, that would materially impact CODA’s operations. Asia, being the main revenue region, is also at risk if the U.S. decides to impose export controls across the globe on materials and products.

Despite these risks, I am very much interested in the company’s future, mainly because I saw very little regarding AI and data centers, which I am already tired of, and I am actively looking to diversify away from them. Therefore, I am going to assign it a buy rating and will look to start a position over the next week, depending on what the week ahead holds. I see quite a bit of potential over the next couple of years. It is a risky bet, but I’d like to make some risky bets from time to time.

data.title: BWX: High Oil Prices Will Translate Into Higher Global Rates (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The Iran war is not a locally contained issue but one that has ripple effects throughout the world's financial system. While as of now an investor can observe high oil prices and increasing costs at the pump, the secondary effects down the line are very substantial and will affect central bank monetary policy in the next year.

A less visible effect of skyrocketing oil prices has been a reset in global rates, with yields spiking higher as market participants realized the Iran conflict might take longer than expected. In today's article we are going to revisit the SPDR Bloomberg International Treasury Bond ETF (BWX), a global bond ETF we covered two years ago. In that article we described the fund composition and its reaction to spiking rates in 2022, as well as our view on the investment vehicle. In today's article we are going to show readers why a prolonged crisis in the Middle East will likely negatively impact BWX via higher global rates.

What does BWX hold?

Let us start by looking at the fund's stated objective:

The Bloomberg Global Treasury ex-US Capped Index is designed to track the fixed-rate local currency sovereign debt of investment grade countries outside the United States. The Index includes government bonds issued by investment grade countries outside the United States, in local currencies, that have a remaining maturity of one year or more and are rated investment grade.

The ETF therefore holds international government bonds that are rated investment grade. The name does not hedge its currency exposure, thus providing for upside in a weak dollar environment. The main geographies currently present in the fund are as follows:

  • Japan: 23.3%
  • UK: 5%
  • France: 4.9%
  • China: 4.7%
  • Canada: 4.6%
  • Germany: 4.6%
  • Spain: 4.5%
  • Italy: 4.5%
  • South Korea: 4.4%
  • Australia: 4%
  • Belgium: 3.7%
  • Netherlands: 2.9%

The ETF has a very wide geographic reach and is diversified. Some analytics for the name are:

  • Number of holdings: 1,671
  • Option-adjusted duration: 7.4 years
  • Portfolio yield to maturity: 3.32%
  • Portfolio OAS: 11.24 bps
  • SEC yield: 2.82%
  • AUM: $1.5 B
  • Expense ratio: 0.35%

Given its investment-grade government build, the fund has very low yields, commensurate with local central bank rate policies. The main driver for gains for the ETF is represented by global rates, and we can see the severe negative impact on the fund in 2022 when yields spiked:

When global yields move higher, the fund goes down in price given its duration of 7.4 years. The current yield is fairly small at 2.26%; thus, duration and global rates are the main profit and loss drivers here. Let us look at how global rates are doing.

Higher oil prices are bringing back the specter of inflation

While investors initially thought the conflict would be a short one, the optics have changed dramatically as of late:

Three weeks later, the prospect of a drawn-out war is causing mounting economic problems. Oil prices have soared above $100 a barrel, European gas prices have doubled, volatility stalks financial markets, and consumers worldwide are bracing for a surge in living costs. Central banks, including the US Federal Reserve, Bank of England and European Central Bank, warn the war could have a material impact on inflation and dent global growth.

Financial markets are not waiting for the end results but have reacted, moving government yields higher:

In the above charts, which present the UK 10-year yields and Japan 10-year rates, we can clearly see the spike higher as the Iran conflict started. While the front end of a yield curve embeds central bank monetary policy, the long end reacts to a multitude of factors, including inflation expectations. When the market believes inflation might start going up again, it moves rates higher in order to be compensated for it.

Although the long end of the curve has already moved for most of the held countries, the front end is following soon, with central banks set to now hike rates:

The quantum of hikes and the net impact on the long end will be determined by the ever-evolving situation in Iran and the ultimate result of the conflict. A prolonged war that results in significant infrastructure destruction and thus forces less volume even after the conflict ends is inflationary in nature. The more the conflict drags on and destroys oil infrastructure, the higher the long end of government curves. The picture is very clear for all central banks:

Top central banks have said they stand ready to tackle any surge in inflation with tighter policy as the latest escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran puts the Middle East's vital energy infrastructure in the line of fire, pushing fuel prices higher. In a rare coincidence of the monetary policy diary, central banks of the United States, Japan, Britain, Canada and the euro zone – effectively the Group of Seven (G7) nations — convened this week, as have counterparts from several emerging economies.

The impact of higher long rates on BWX was immediate:

The fund has had a -3% drawdown, basically accounting for a year of dividends. Do expect the name to move lower if the resolution to the conflict is not fast and swift.

Risks are skewed to the downside

At the current juncture we are witnessing a geopolitical situation without a clear resolution, where the goals of the Iran conflict are not readily transparent. There are constant updates on the war from both sides, with conflicting messages. The fear here is a long conflict that will keep oil prices high, which in turn will create a massive inflationary wave:

Brent oil is up an astounding +69% this year, creating the premise for significant price pressures across a large segment of goods:

The above chart from Goldman does a nice job of quantifying the impact on headline inflation under a base case and adverse scenarios. In the Euro Area, for example, we can see inflation moving up by 3% in the most adverse scenario.

As an investor, you want to buy assets that offer good rewards for the risk taken and sell those that have more downside than upside. BWX offers a mere 2.8% SEC yield and the potential for another leg down in price if the Iran conflict does not end swiftly. We are a 'Sell' for the name here.

Conclusion

BWX is a fixed-income exchange-traded fund. The name is an aggregator for global government bonds that hold an investment-grade rating. The fund is negatively impacted by higher global rates and has seen its price decrease on the back of the Iran war. The longer the conflict lasts, the higher the global inflationary pressures, thus putting pressure on rates and bond prices. With risks skewed to the downside here and a low 2.8% SEC yield, we are a 'Sell' for BWX in the current macro.

data.title: The Shape Of Oil/Energy Shipping: Looking At Frontline
data.description:
It's fair to say that shipping, especially the transport and shipping of oil/energy, is one of the more volatile businesses that there is to be invested in and to look at. Over the course of the start of the Iran war, I've slowly researched more and become a bit more comfortable with potentially investing in the sector. I haven't done so yet, but I may do so in the future.

Part of the reason is that energy shipping is fairly "close to home" for me. Many of the companies, even the one I am covering today, are actually owned by businesses or businessmen with ties or origins in Scandinavia. Many of them are even listed at "home" on our stock markets. This one is an example of such a company.

Frontline plc (FRO) has both an NYSE ticker, FRO, and an Oslo ticker in the NOK FX, OSE: FRO. This means that it's extremely appealing on a basis of FX diversification for those available for investing here, while also allowing U.S. investors to get exposure to what essentially amounts to a comparatively small business in energy.

I say small in terms of net income and revenue compared to other companies listed on the stock market, not small in terms of its size.

If you want to invest in these companies, you need to leave traditional notions of value investing behind and focus on the shorter term and understanding industries/sectors. That's the biggest challenge I have with this entire sector. You need to watch commodity cycle trends, and you need to be comfortable forecasting a lot more and reacting/estimating when those forecasts aren't materialized. You also need to have a long-term holding period. Because, as we've seen in the past, shipping can stay down for years - sometimes a decade. That does not mean that the assets you own shares in are bad or worth selling.

If you look at the valuation of Frontline, you'll find very little that makes sense to you unless you understand the sector.

In this article, I will attempt to provide you with some basic understanding and show you how I think about the sector and about Frontline.

Frontline - Upside From Shipping in This Environment

Frontline is a PLC, not an ASA. This means that the company is not incorporated in Norway but in a common law jurisdiction - in this case, Cyprus - even if Norwegian law, due to its financing, dictates some of its major moves (and with a management company incorporated in Bermuda). It has a market cap (Norway) of nearly 70B NOK for the ticker; for the U.S., it has assets of about $5.8B (mostly tied up in vessels) and revenues per year of about $2B. These give them a net income between $380M and $650M in good years, which can go negative in bad years, as you see below.

Frontline's origins is originally a Swedish company, but about 30 years ago it was bought up by (meaning the largest shareholder), Billionaire John Fredriksen. The company has gone and absorbed multiple shipping companies, such as London and Overseas almost directly after his takeover, ICB, and Golden Ocean.

This has made Frontline one of the largest tanker companies - a position it still holds in 2026.

Frontline is a very solidly capitalized company. By this, I mean that when I analyze the balance sheet, I find very little material or objective weakness. With over $700M in cash and equivalents and no significant debt maturing for the next 4 years, the company is in no immediate worries no matter what happens.

The last few years have been active for Frontline. The company has been both selling and buying new assets/renovations. In 2025 (4Q report), the company sold 8 VLCCs for over $800M, which, net of debt, generated almost $480M in cash proceeds - taking advantage of a currently pressured market while at the same time improving its own appeal by buying ECO VLCC newbuildings at close to $1.25B.

The company's fleet, as of today, consists of 41 VLCCs, 21 Suezmaxes, and 18 LR2s. The company is in the process of fitting scrubbers and ESG on these, where this is not done.

The company has very attractive breakeven rates. This "breakeven" rate represents what the company has to earn (minimum) in freight to cover expenses. The company's breakeven rates for its VLCCs are $25,000 per day, and this is the highest. To give you a comparison, recent conflicts have inflated Atlantic freight to over $60,000 per day,-and I don't see this going down anytime soon.

In a good operating environment, which we technically have right now, FRO does very well for itself. It's a cash-generative business, as evidenced by over $660M in FCF in the last reported fiscal year. However, it's also incredibly volatile in this (and other metrics), as we can easily see on a historical basis.

It's fair to say that Frontline plc goes up and down with the trends in the industry. Now, FCF might not be the best metric to gauge this company, but given the recent spike, I at least wanted to show this to you. The reason why FCF is a sub-par metric is due to the extreme capital intensity of the business. Frontline can be doing very well, and FCF may still look poor. Instead, we need to turn our eyes to the commodities market.

It's fair to say that demand for shipping is on the rise. It's further fair to say that the demand for shipping will continue to rise. Oil transportation companies with a modern, EU-attractive fleet are in a prime position to take advantage of this market. The market is underserved by newbuilds, which is why the company is asset recycling, with asset prices firmly going up. It will be some time before things go "down" here.

The geopolitical volatility that seems to be characterizing the current macro, not only in Iran but elsewhere as well, is fertile ground for Frontline PLC to generate profits. Global crude oil in transit continues to be higher than it has been for a long time, and imports, both globally but above all in the Far East/India, continue to go up. Exports, before the war, were going up or at high levels from OPEC - now that Iran is blocking Hormuz with no immediate end in sight, these tailwinds have intensified.

Looking at shipbuilder order books, most of the orders until 2029 are concerning Containers, Bulkers and LNG - crude tankers aren't on the rise until 2029 and beyond. Provided the market remains supportive, the demand for VLCC (Very Large Crude Carriers), is expected to not only be maintained, but to rise further.

Overall, I like what I see. I may be somewhat of a newcomer investor in the tanker/shipping market, but I know how to analyze good fundamentals. The company has good fundamentals. You have to realize, of course, that Frontline doesn't care for your "dividend tradition." It pays out what it can, and it doesn't care how the dividend level moves. In 2022 the company dividend was 1.52 NOK. In 2024, it was 21 NOK. For 2026, it's now expected to be 54.56 NOK. The company decides, strictly based on profit, how much it pays out. Given the capital intensity, this is exactly what they should be doing.

With its current fleet size operating 365, the company has 30,000+ days of earnings per year, and if you look at what the daily cost of a tanker is today, you can quickly add that up to a lot of cash generation. What's more, FRO is a net beneficiary of sanctions and tariffs.

Why?

Because they lower the utilization of assets, bringing forth Frontline's compliant assets.

The so-called compliant crude is the oil that's up. Sanctioned crude has been flat for a long time, with Iran's the only one showing even slight growth (before the war, obviously).

So, Frontline is volatile - yes. But Frontline is also probably one of the most appealing companies in this entire sector, if you're so inclined to invest. They combine what I consider to be extremely safe, well-managed operations with what can only be described as low levels of debt (less than 50% of equity). And while FCF and EPS may be volatile, there's one metric that has stayed positive through every market - OCF. For over 10 years, FRO has managed positive operational cash flow, which has been rising more and more as the company's assets and balance sheet has improved.

Let me, therefore, show you how I would value the company at this time.

Valuing Frontline PLC - A Tricky Venture, but Far From Impossible

Shipping companies are valued using a mix of method with a high degree of certainty (or at least, higher), with methods that are more speculative. There's unfortunately no helping this, no matter how "certain" some analysts want to seem in their forecasts.

The simpler method with high certainty is NAV. We look at net asset value because the ships are the assets (minus debt), and these make up what the company "is." The only uncertainty in this method is value appreciation for the vessels, which goes to market value trends, and asset age. Both of these factors are more manageable (infinitely so) than the other approach.

Because the other approach is usually an income-based method. Unfortunately, as we've established, forecasting a company's cash flow or earnings on charter rates is fairly likened to using a crystal ball. Because of the volatility in these sectors, I've taken an approach that more heavily leans towards the former, because this is the method we can actually control. The most recent numbers here suggest assets of $5.8B, which, net of debt, come to about $2.5B in equity (and adding cash back), which comes to a net asset value per share of about $12.4/share as of the company's full-year filings. Based on a current share price of ~$33/share, it operates currently significantly above its NAV, but it was less than a year ago, that it operated close to NAV, at below $14.5/share.

This spike reflects the estimated surge in earnings for 2026.

This is basically the market "guessing" what will happen during the year. Current price targets, or PTs, reflect this. The PT about 1.5 years ago was less than $27.5/share. Now the average, from 4 analysts, is $41.25/share with a high-end target of $46/share. This reflects the high cyclicality. Going by market valuations of NAV (S&P Global), the company has gone between lows of 0.36x and highs of close to 2x, currently at 1.17x.

The trick here is finding a good middle ground between the currently significant surging targets due to the fundamentals in the sector, and the more conservative long-term trends that we can see for between 2010 and 2020. I would argue that, based on the company's fleet renewals and upgrades, Frontline is in a prime position even going forward. I have little issue allowing a bit of a premium for the company.

Because of achieving high TCE earnings in Q4 ($74,200 per day for the VLCC), with a very low fleet age (100% eco-friendly, less than 8 years average age), a very limited market, a very conservative capital allocation policy, and solid (as I see it) management, I raise my expectations for the Q1 '26 earnings. I believe carrier rates are likely to be sustained. The company is trading at a significant premium in P/E to most of its peers - over 18x based on 2025, compared to 13x-15x average, but the combination of continued high spot rates with a very attractive fleet makes a strong case for this being valid.

Put another way, I don't believe 2026 will be a bad year for earnings. And if it's not bad, it's likely to be good - very good. While I will discount the current estimate of $6/share by at least 20%, that still puts us at a 2026E P/E of 8.25x - undervalued at $33/share. Even if we normalize the coming 3 years at 40-50% lower than 2026E, which I believe to be fair, that brings us to a 3-year average of $3.33/share, or, based on $33/share, a P/E of about 10x.

That is technically undervalued.

Even if I further discount the PT and assumptions, I get to a 20% annualized rate of return (with the estimated 9-11% dividend yield based on $3.33 EPS) until 2028, which justifies a PT of $34.5/share.

That makes the company, as it stands today, a Buy - if only a slim one.

Thesis

Frontline PLC is a net beneficiary of the current market trends. It has one of the most modern tanker fleets in the world, and is also one of the largest companies in its field, in the entire world. While I typically am careful about covering this, and I do view this company as a speculative investment, I also view it as attractive here.

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $34.5/share price target. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a Buy rating, but would also make it clear that this is a speculative rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $34.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of March 2026.

data.title: SkyWater Technology Shows Strong Revenue Gains But Profitability Remains Weak
data.description:
My view on SkyWater Technology (SKYT) has shifted since I last rated it. Back then, I called it a Hold. I was worried about how much the company needed to execute just right and I thought the big growth hopes were mostly priced in already. At that time, I figured 2026 would be the important test. The stock had already tripled off the April lows, with about over 80% growth over the year. I said, “today’s price already reflects a lot of that middle-to-bullish scenario. So there’s real downside risk if the execution isn’t almost perfect… I’d want to see at least a couple more quarters of good, steady results before feeling more confident about the stock”.

A few things have changed since then. The stock backed up some of the more optimistic stories, hitting record consolidated revenue for FY2025, $442.1 million, up 29% from the year before. Also, SkyWater actually pulled off some things that used to be big question marks. The Fab 25 integration, which looked risky at first, ended up better than expected in both finances and operations. It brought in $175.6 million in revenue in its first full year for SkyWater. That’s not just a nice bump; it’s a shift in scale. Now SkyWater is the biggest US pure-play foundry at the basic chip nodes, with its share of the 200mm foundry market estimated to have increased from roughly 4% to about 17%.

Profitability Pressure

Still, my earlier caution sticks when it comes to profits and how reliable they’ll be. Non-GAAP gross margin for 2025 was 20.7%. Adjusted EBITDA for the full year came in at $53.2 million, or a 12% margin, up from 10% last year. But the actual bottom line? Still pretty thin: non-GAAP net income was just $0.7 million, about $0.01 per diluted share. On the official (GAAP) numbers, profits were skewed by one-time gains from the Fab 25 deal. What really stands out: revenues from the original SkyWater business went down 22% during this period. That’s a big drop hiding under all the new revenue from Fab 25 and it’s not something you can just ignore. The old part of the business is shrinking, and while quantum and advanced packaging are growing, they’re still smaller and haven’t proven they can deliver strong enough margins to pull the whole company out of the low-to-mid teens zone.

Valuation and Balance Sheet

Valuation’s still kind of a hang-up for going all-in on a Buy here. Market cap sits at about $1.3 billion and if you look at the forward revenue estimate, around $600 million based on company guidance, the price-to-sales ratio comes out to about 2.2. Forward EV/EBITDA is up at about 23.5, which is a lot higher than what some peers usually see and forward P/E is still in the red because they keep posting net losses. Peer sales multiples aren’t totally out of whack for a US-based foundry with high-growth stuff going on, but the fact that they haven’t nailed down consistent profits and have ongoing working capital headaches makes it hard to say the stock is really cheap. Cash flow from operations for the whole year was negative, at approximately $(24.1) million. The company also carries a meaningful debt load and relies on financing facilities, so the balance sheet doesn’t give them much cushion if they mess up execution or if government and quantum spending slows down. If that happens, they’ll probably have to raise more money and could end up diluting existing shareholders.

Growth Drivers

SkyWater’s “domestic and trusted” angle has definitely helped them win business in quantum, government and aerospace. Management is now saying they see at least $600 million in 2026 revenue, mostly off the back of momentum at Fab 25 and a steady ramp in quantum deals, ATS quantum-related revenue jumped over 30% in 2025. The advanced packaging ramp beat Q4 expectations for both ATS and Tools revenue. Still, the company isn’t turning all that gross profit dollar growth into real free cash flow yet. High capex and some trouble folding everything together are dragging things down.

Operational Risks

A lot could still go wrong here, so it’s hard to get too excited. Even management says timing on government contracts is unpredictable, and scaling up Fab 25 might run into cost overruns. Inflation showed up in a real way in Q4, tooling installs in Florida cost $9.3 million more than expected. That hit the gross margin for the quarter and just drove home how messy operations can get, and you can’t really see all the bumps ahead or hedge them away. If there’s a big slowdown, or if they lose or don’t renew some key quantum or government contracts, sales multiples could drop, and the company would have to deal with already tight leverage and cash.

Investors are still really optimistic right now, with headlines focused on strong results and momentum in quantum, along with Fab 25 performing better than earlier expectations. But after the stock more than quadrupled since April, it’s hard to see the multiple getting any higher unless future upgrades are backed by steady, real profit. Management says they’re aiming for $600 million or more in revenue by 2026, and maybe that’s doable. And with the company filing to potentially raise $350 million through mixed securities in 2025, it looks like they’re leaving the door open to getting more cash if things get tight or if another big scaling opportunity comes up.

IonQ Deal

The $1.8 billion IonQ deal, cash and stock has become a key factor in how investors are valuing the stock, with some treating it partly as a merger-driven situation rather than purely a standalone growth story. But the deal isn’t done. It still needs regulatory and shareholder approval. The company even said, “the inability to consummate the acquisition of SkyWater by IonQ… could cause the Company’s actual results… to be materially different,” which just means if the deal blows up or drags out, the stock could get real volatile.

Hold Rating

I’m sticking with a Hold on SkyWater Technology. Even though they’ve made progress operationally and the revenue quality looks better since Fab 25, the risk/reward at this price doesn’t make sense with the margin swings, ongoing cash burn and the usual headaches from integrating and dealing with market cycles. They have to actually show they can get to 20%-plus non-GAAP gross margins, turn in positive EPS each quarter and really flip free cash flow so it covers all their growth and deal-making. That’s what it’ll take for me to bump my rating.

Right now, both the story and the numbers need to be checked out independently. The market’s still treating this like a growth play, but if even one part of the bullish case cracks, a lot of people could get caught wrong-footed. If you’re being careful, it probably makes sense to wait unless SkyWater puts together several strong quarters in a row, with good cash from operations and margins above 20% non-GAAP, along with steady revenue beats. Until then, I just don’t see enough upside for the risk to make this a Buy.

data.title: The Trade Desk: This Is Getting Ridiculous
data.description:
Late last year, I triple upgraded The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) from a sell to a strong buy rating. I believe there was a generational buying opportunity since the long term growth story remained intact and management demonstrated confidence through aggressive share buybacks. As shown in the chart below, The Trade Desk has slid another ~40% since then and so if the fundamentals and outlook have remained unchanged, we may be looking at the bargain of the century. Today, we'll analyze their most recent results and look at some recent news to see whether this is in fact the case right now.

Below, it is shown that the company is indeed feeling the effects of geopolitical and macro headwinds. Top line growth continues to slow while guidance for 2026 Q1 is also soft. While an uncertain backdrop has created significant near term risks, the long term remains bright in my view. Much of the international market remains untapped for The Trade Desk and their potential OpenAI deal signals that their business remains very relevant in the age of AI. Meanwhile, the valuation has continued to slide and so at current levels I view the risk/reward as asymmetrically favorable for the bulls. Therefore, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating on the stock.

Global Expansion Potential

Before we talk about the numbers specific to their 2025 Q4, it is important to look at the big picture opportunity moving forward. The above map is a slide from their investor presentation that does a good job at summing up the growth potential for The Trade Desk. As stated above, 86% of their revenues come from North America while 60% of the world's ad dollars are spent outside North America. Therefore, their business is still very much concentrated in the domestic market.

While they do have global presence with offices around the world, in terms of their revenues, I would say that their potential to grow globally is immense right now and moving forward. It is important to keep this long term opportunity in mind even if the immediate financials aren't the most pretty currently.

Ad Targeting Is Key

At the end of the day, businesses will spend on ads if that fuels a meaningful increase in demand. While we are currently in a bout of consumer weakness, in the long run, precise ad targeting is key. In today's age of AI, the improvements to targeting are significant. For example, investors that monitor Meta's (META) ad metrics know that they have strong impressions data as they have successfully integrated AI.

For The Trade Desk, you can see above that they have extensive efforts to strengthen targeting and that is much needed to stay competitive in the digital ads world today. If the company is able to achieve robust targeting numbers, I believe the likelihood of global expansion success would be strong.

Growth Continues To Sink

For 2025 Q4, The Trade Desk generated total revenues of $847 million. While a growth rate of 14.27% isn't really that bad, it does represent a third consecutive deceleration. This multiyear low in growth is a potential concern but it should be noted that in Founder and CEO Jeff Green's words, the company "executed against a backdrop of macro uncertainty." Therefore, this top line weakness can probably be attributed to weak consumer spending rather than real issues with the company itself.

I would say that it is quite clear in the above chart that the declines in revenue growth aligns with when tariff policy was enacted and so The Trade Desk has been feeling considerable headwinds it seems. Lastly, despite this weakness, they still beat top line expectations by a decent margin of $6.11 million and so Q4 was better than feared.

Adjusted EBITDA Margin Holds Steady

In my previous article on the company, I found adjusted EBITDA expansion of around 2 percentage points as a indication of operational improvement. As shown in the table above, for Q4, there was no such improvement as the metric remained unchanged YoY at 47%. While an expansion in margin is always even more favorable, investors should still give them credit for the resilience they have demonstrated.

Amid a less than favorable macro backdrop, holding adjusted EBITDA margin in the high 40s percentage range isn't such a bad thing in my view. For the bottom line, they saw basically no growth for adjusted EPS YoY but they still beat analyst expectations by $0.01 and so again, things aren't so bad for profitability in the grand scheme of things.

Buybacks Remain Aggressive

For Q4, The Trade Desk continued their aggressive capital return policy and so it seems that management remains confident about their cash position and the value of their stock. In the quarter, they bought back $423 million in Class A common stock, up from $310 million back in Q3 and just $57 million in the year ago quarter. You can see above that buybacks exceeded their FCFs for Q4 and so they are cutting into their cash pile.

As of the end of 2025, they have $658.175 million in cash and cash equivalents but they have significant balances of other current assets that can help to fund future share repurchases should they decide to continue with their current capital return policy. As another sign of management confidence, it was reported just earlier this month that CEO Green bought $148 million of stock. Insider buying is quite a strong signal that the stock maybe undervalued for what it is right now.

Financial Guidance

The Trade Desk provided some guidance for their 2026 Q1. They see revenues of at least $678 million. That would translate into YoY growth of 10% or more. Since the top line saw a 14% increase back in Q4, there is a good likelihood that we may see another quarter of deceleration. In my view, this is not overly surprising given that the macro backdrop continues to be unfavorable. They did miss consensus expectations of $688.37 million.

As for adjusted EBITDA, they are expecting around $195 million. This implies a 6% decline. This is of course a potential concern since it indicates that adjusted EBITDA margin will shift to contraction in Q1. As a whole, near term guidance is indeed soft as both business activity and profitability are set to weaken moving forward.

OpenAI Deal?

Before we talk about the valuation, I want to mention some developments on the AI side of things. Firstly, the company had its own innovation strategy and that is reflected in their AI ambitions. In their earnings call, CEO Green stated that

Almost 100% of our clients are running through Kokai today. We think Kokai is the most advanced AI-fueled buying platform ever pointed at the open Internet.

Therefore, The Trade Desk is indeed integrating AI into their solutions to improve the experience for customers. This should reassure investors that they remain in an innovative state.

A major development that has come up not long ago would be the potential ad deal with OpenAI. By now it is no secret that OpenAI CEO Sam Altman is actively trying to introduce ads on ChatGPT and so a collaboration with The Trade Desk is really a logical move. While there haven't been reports of real commitments yet, The Trade Desk did soar 21% pre-market when the news came out. In my view, even if this was a bit of an overreaction, it does seem the company has all the ingredients to be successful in this age of AI powered digital marketing.

Valuation Is Laughable

Back in my previous article, The Trade Desk's forward P/E ratio was at 21.95 and was near multiyear lows. As you can see in the chart above, the multiple has continued its steep slide and is now at just 10.69. I believe this has created an asymmetric risk/reward profile that is in favor of the bulls.

Firstly, it is important to recognize that the geopolitical and macro-economic picture is indeed a risk for the company. The war in the Middle East and the continued tariff uncertainty are likely to weigh on consumer spending in the near future and so further sluggishness and weakness is likely. Their financial guidance for Q1 is softer than desired and in my view there is potential that this tough period could stretch out significantly further than that. Therefore, if the stock continues to react negatively to near term results, more declines are possible.

For long term investors, I believe this is truly a generational buying opportunity. The global ad market is sizeable and that offers The Trade Desk significant room to expand. Through improved ad targeting and AI integration, the company seems to be in position to capture a good part of this market. Their potential deal with OpenAI shows that they remain as relevant as ever and so at a forward P/E of just 10.69, the stock is severely undervalued in my view.

Conclusion

With the stock down over 50% in just the past six months and the forward P/E down to nearly single digits territory, I believe the market's mis-pricing of The Trade Desk has gotten to ridiculous levels. As stated above, there is no doubt that there is a flurry of near term risks but this short-termism has created asymmetric opportunity for long term investors. The company's business model remains highly intact and there isn't much to suggest that their financials won't restrengthen once headwinds dissipate. With the stock continuing to be a steal, I have decided to reiterate my strong buy rating at this juncture.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Platform And Hardware Strength To Face AI Disruption
data.description:
Since the last time I covered Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) in March 2023, it has gained more than 70% and was trading around $166 at the time of writing, as shown below. At that time, I was bullish because of the breadth of its portfolio and its ability to provide end-to-end solutions.

However, the stock has been highly volatile since November last year, initially because of AI bubble concerns, but later it was negatively impacted by disruption fears after AI startup Anthropic (ANTHRO) demonstrated how easy it was to create applications that normally take weeks to be built by software and cybersecurity companies employing armies of developers.

This thesis aims to show that the market has punished the stock too hard, given that it has continued to broaden its portfolio through new acquisitions, while offering new products through its platform, as part of its platformization strategy. Another reason for being bullish is that while disruption fears are real, as I explain below, Palo Alto appears relatively more insulated because it also sells hardware products compared to pure-play software cybersecurity providers.

Explaining AI-Related Disruption, which has nonetheless been less Brutal for Palo Alto

The main fear surrounding disruption started to emerge around January 13 when AI startup Anthropic released the Cowork tool, based on the Claude LLM or large language model. This AI agent showed that non-specialists can perform certain software development tasks that currently require professional effort. Subsequently, Claude released some plugins (or connectors for accessing third-party applications) on January 30, which enhanced Cowork capabilities in some of the areas where software development companies get paid to create applications. This meant competition, and the market reaction was brutal towards both software and cybersecurity, as shown in the chart below, with the phenomenon termed the "SaaS apocalypse."

Specifically for cybersecurity, investors seem concerned that AI agents may help in writing the software code for detecting viruses, analyzing threats, and even responding to incidents. Consequently, this could pose a threat to the business of IT security companies that are currently responsible for such operations.

As for Palo Alto, which also suffered from the selloff, it has recovered faster than SentinelOne (S), as seen above. The reason, as I explain below, is that disruption risks are higher for pure-play software cybersecurity like SentinelOne, compared to Palo Alto, which also offers hardware solutions, meaning it is relatively insulated against AI disruption.

Palo Alto's Hardware Insulation, Platformization Strength, and Embedding AI into Products

In this connection, contrary to software, which can be easily downloaded from the internet and rapidly erased in case a company switches suppliers, hardware items like firewalls (a shield against hackers) are physically present in a data center or network cabinet. Consequently, replacing a firewall is a heavy task since it involves physical installation, cabling work, and disrupting network operations. Also, one has to factor in capital expenditure in sharp contrast to subscription-based software.

Thus, hardware means higher switching costs than software. I believe that even if a startup comes up with an AI-driven firewall, a company would have to go through the entire procurement and physical deployment process before switching. On the other hand, cloud-native solution provider SentinelOne is more vulnerable because, while still requiring some extensive technical processes, changing is easier as its products are just software.

Furthermore, Palo Alto's pivot towards platformization since April 2024, as shown below, or move away from selling hardware boxes and discrete software to instead bundling everything into a platform, also constitutes a strength. In this way, the hardware often serves as the entry point to their broader suite of software services (Strata, Prisma, or Cortex), meaning once a client buys a firewall, it is also subscribing to the cloud-based threat intelligence services, or a recurring software revenue stream that runs on Palo Alto's platform.

Pursuing further, instead of being disrupted, the company has innovated by enhancing its product offerings through AI. This is exemplified by Palo Alto Networks using Google's (GOOG) Gemini LLM to power its own chatbots and optimize its internal services using Generative AI. Additionally, to counter the increasing sophistication of hackers, it has come up with innovative tools like Prisma AIRS to protect LLM-driven apps.

This reminds us that hackers are growing more sophisticated to the point of "weaponizing AI agents at scale" as per Palo Alto's CEO during the first quarter of fiscal year 2026 (FQ1-26) earnings call. To counteract this threat, he emphasized how his company's centralized approach to cybersecurity (by providing services through its platform) is better than a more fragmented one.

Now, embracing innovation has resulted in more recurrent sales, as I discuss later, but first, I highlight risks.

There is Competition, but Palo Alto is Technically Superior and is Less Costly

A report by Dan Yves from Wedbush Securities about the negative impact of higher prices of components like DRAMs (computer memory), which are used in firewalls produced by the likes of Palo Alto and Check Point Software Technologies (CHKP). Now, this may reduce their profit margins, or worse, the unavailability of certain critical items may impact deployment schedules and sales forecasts.

Investigating further, both companies are top players in the network security space, but Palo Alto is better than Check Point for both prevention and response capabilities, as pictured below. Another of its advantages at a time of rising inflation is that its products cost less for the client in terms of TCO (total cost of ownership), thanks to bundling rather than relying on discrete product sales. Additionally, it provides volume discounts tied to long-term commitments to government agencies, which can lower the upfront cost of hardware appliances.

Moreover, Check Point could be more at risk since it has not embarked on platformization to the same scale as Palo Alto. Thus, it is not in a position to bundle its firewalls with other products in such a way as to land more recurring subscription revenue.

Looking deeper, Palo Alto has been able to offer such platform capabilities thanks to acquisitions of Cortex XSIAM (security operations) and Prisma AIRS (for AI and cloud security). At the same time, integration of new capabilities has been key to enabling consolidated (end-to-end) solutions. Furthermore, as the cybersecurity landscape gets scarier with Claude's agentic AI capabilities helping hackers on their evil missions, it recently acquired Koi to address the related security risks to further augment its platform.

Deserves a better Valuation due to more Platformization Deals

As a result, Palo Alto reported 1,550 platformization deals in FQ2-26, up by 35% YoY. Related, net retention for platformized customers was 119%, a metric showing strong loyalty and expanding usage. In other words, customers have been spending relatively more dollars on its products.

Also, ARR for NGS (Next-Generation Security) surged to $6.33 billion, growing 28% YoY, benefiting from demand from customers protecting their AI deployments. In addition, NGS also includes identity and access management solutions, which have traditionally been a fragmented market, but where Palo Alto's unified approach provides better protection against the proliferation of AI agents, as touched upon above and pictured below.

However, putting things into perspective, such growth driven by providing discounts and aggressively acquiring new expertise has resulted in a lower net income margin compared to Check Point for two reasons. First, by offering potential customers free trials to sway them away from competitors, Palo Alto is willing to incur high customer acquisition costs. Second, more frequent acquisitions mean higher integration costs. This means a willingness to sacrifice margins to win additional market share in the enterprise security domain.

Still, as shown above, its free cash flow margins are higher, despite the company spending more capital on acquisitions, showing clients paying upfront in cash (including the firewall costs). Now, FCF is expected to surge from 29% to 37% (adjusted) in FY-26, or by around 28%. Now, assuming the forward P/FCF remains constant at 28x, then the price could scale by this amount.

Moderating the Upside in View of Risks

However, the company is not completely immune to AI disruption risks since it also sells software solutions, namely, software firewalls. In this respect, instead of expanding their subscriptions with Palo Alto, some firms can use AI specifically for scanning activities, whereby lines of code are checked for malicious intent. Here, Claude's ability to detect complex flaws during anomaly detection needs to be highlighted. Also, hyperscalers offering IT security, namely Microsoft (MSFT) and Google, can bundle features like identity management in their product suite, posing a direct competitive threat to Palo Alto.

This may, in turn, impact the revenue generated per user account, causing the FCF to grow less than expected. Thus, moderating the potential upside to 14% or half of the 28% identified above, I obtain a target of $189-$190 (166x1.14), based on its current share price of $166. This is roughly its price before the Anthropic-led AI disruption fears gripped the market.

In conclusion, I have a buy position because the company's platform delivers integrated solutions, and its hardware products provide some insulation against disruption. To further support my optimism, especially at a time when AI is accentuating the number of cyberattacks, it is unlikely for corporations to divert resources away from their core business towards "experimenting" with new threat detection mechanisms championed by AI startups, which may work well for certain tasks, but are largely inappropriate for end-to-end protection of corporate IT assets. This is where Palo Alto already delivers.

Finally, faced with the prospect of an AI agent going rogue and causing havoc in their IT systems in the same way as in spy movies, it is strongly likely for corporations to prioritize the human trust factor, which only established cybersecurity providers can bring in the age of growing complexity and accelerated automation.

data.title: Palantir: This Selloff Is A Gift
data.description:
Performance assessment

Palantir (PLTR) initially corrected after I published my last update on the stock, but it has rebounded this month:

Elevator pitch

I am becoming more bullish again on Palantir. Here's the thesis:

  1. Maven AI's success can secure a long term stream of federal contracts
  2. Strong hiring trends in the Palantir ecosystem are a sign of robust growth ahead
  3. Economies of scale can lead to massive profit margin expansion
  4. PLTR stock valuations are lower but the growth ask is still high
  5. Bulls look ready to resume the uptrend

Maven AI's success can secure a long term stream of federal contracts

Palantir's Maven Smart System is an AI platform that fuses satellite, drone, and sensor data for battlefield analysis in real-time applications. Yesterday's news update revealed that this system is now an official program of record, which means this piece of work is now a formally approved, budgeted, long‑term acquisition program for the Pentagon. This is an upgrade that Palantir has secured in less than a year from a mere pilot or experimental contract. Here's what the key decision maker said about why the US Department of Defense have made Maven AI a core program:

embedding Palantir’s Maven Smart System would provide warfighters with 'the latest tools necessary to detect, deter, and dominate our adversaries in all domains.' The move would lock in long-term use of the company's weapons-targeting technology in the U.S. military...

- Deputy Secretary of Defense Steve Feinberg, Seeking Alpha, Reuters

Naturally, this is a very bullish development for Palantir as it positions them to win more deals across the US and in my view even foreign defense departments in a more streamlined way:

Designating PLTR's Maven Smart System as a program of record will streamline the company's deals across all branches of the military while providing long-term funding to Palantir, with future contracting being handled by the US Army

- Wedbush Securities Analyst Dan Ives

From a numbers perspective, I believe this can fuel further growth acceleration in government revenues:

Strong hiring trends in the Palantir ecosystem are a sign of robust growth ahead

I did a LinkedIn search for job postings at Palantir and Northslope Technologies (a top Palantir implementation partner):

The high, broad-based recruiting posts for forward deployed engineers is a sign of a lot of growth in technical work in Palantir's ecosystem. But what is also noteworthy is the fact that there have been 15 total postings in the People & Recruiting function. Basically, these companies are hiring more recruiters. When companies do this, I think it is generally a very bullish sign indicating rapid expansion.

Ultimately, I believe this alternative data gives some very favorable indications that the sustainability of PLTR's growth is still extremely robust and only improving. Hence, I expect continued revenue growth acceleration to follow.

Economies of scale can lead to massive profit margin expansion

In investing, DCF models in theory are the best way to value a company. However, in practice the issue that makes implementation difficult and messy is that the output insights of DCF valuation depend a lot on the accuracy of the input data and assumptions. I posit that the same applies to real-world uses of AI; a lot depends on the accuracy and comprehensiveness of the input data.

Palantir is perfectly positioned to fill enterprises' needs for high quality input data to feed into AI algorithms to deliver highly productive outcomes such as completing ERP migrations that used to take 2 years in just 2 weeks. And the underlying engine powering all this is the labor-intensive, painstaking work Palantir has done over decades on building a data ontology across enterprises:

By bringing together disparate sources of enterprise data (ERP, CRM, databases, sensors, documents, etc.) and mapping them to the real-world entities and events that constitute an organization’s business, relationships and actions among entities and events can be modeled or defined. Importantly, the core elements of the Ontology (objects and properties) are linked directly to the relevant enterprise data sources which are continuously updated. Consequently, building applications, automations, and agents becomes much easier once a thoroughly defined Ontology is in place.

- Morgan Stanley analyst Sanjit Singh's description of Palantir's Ontology

The beautiful thing about Palantir's Ontology-backed implementation of its AI platform [AIP] is that once deployed in organizations, building new applications on top of the Ontology-based stack is very scalable. This is why the incremental YoY normalized PAT margins are so high at close to 70%:

The way to interpret this is to see the incremental PAT margins as an upper ceiling to underlying PAT margins. Given this, I believe some analyst estimates of mid-50%s margins are not only reasonable, but even too low. I believe the company can hit 60% normalized PAT margins by the end of FY27:

PLTR stock valuations are lower but the growth ask is still high

PLTR stock is trading at a 1-yr fwd PE of 122x, which is 477% higher than the software sector median multiple of 21.1x:

Of course, this kind of 1-yr fwd PE multiple looks very high and expensive. But that is normal for a high growth company like Palantir. What I find more useful is noting the fact that the relative premium of PLTR vs its peers has dropped sharply:

Also notice that the underlying earnings growth expectations are still ripping up, but the market has assigned a lower valuation multiple for the stock:

This is great news as it means we can buy a fundamentally better PLTR stock at a cheaper valuation. In recent coverage of Palantir, I have started to share reverse DCF-style implied earnings growth assumptions to get an absolute sense of the stock's valuations. Updating for the latest 10-yr yield, US equity risk premiums and PLTR stock market cap, we still get a high growth ask of a 32.7% earnings 12-yr CAGR, which I admit is the main risk for the stock:

I encourage you to read more about the methodology and logic of this implied earnings growth CAGR approach here.

Bulls look ready to resume the uptrend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

PLTR Technical Analysis

On the monthly chart of PLTR, I see an overall uptrend and bulls defending a key resistance-turned-support level after a few months of a pullback correction. I think it is reasonable to expect further upside to follow from here on.

Takeaway & Positioning

Here's how I'm putting the various pieces together to form a bullish view on Palantir:

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to relentless signs of growth acceleration and ample scope for PAT margin expansion
  • Valuation: Bearish as the growth ask in the valuations are very high (but note that there is a precedent for growing into such valuations), despite a recent valuation multiple correction
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish as the uptrend looks ready to resume
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, 'Buy'

data.title: Evercore Rises Above Market Fears To Be A Leading Fee-Driven Financial Firm
data.description:
Today, I'm initiating coverage of financial stock Evercore (EVR) for the first time, as it is a great moment to take a deeper dive into some major financial firms, with this one in particular having beaten its last 4 earnings results, the most recent one in early February.

From its company website, the firm calls itself "a premier global independent investment banking firm," while also mentioning its work in other segments like equities research, wealth and investment management, among others.

Notably, its wealth management arm, Evercore Wealth Management, has around ~$15.5B in AUM.

Read on to learn more about how I reviewed this stock across 8 rating categories, and how I came up with a holistic rating score while also considering key peers, fundamentals, and technicals too.

Thesis Summary

For my initial coverage of Evercore, I gave it a buy rating, agreeing with the Wall St consensus rating as of Monday.

The rating worksheet below shows how 8 different rating categories contributed to the overall average score:

My article today shows that despite a challenging macro environment for many sectors right now, as well as some bearish technical indicators for this stock, many fundamental strengths remain and that pushes the needle into the buy column for me.

In particular, Evercore saw strong performance vs key peers, has a robust fee-driven business model that is diversified, a compelling dividend story and balance sheet risk profile, impressive operating margins, deals and client inflows, and share price forecasts expecting considerable upside by next year.

Macro & Sector Outlook

When it comes to the macro and sector outlook in the near-term, I am inclined to say hold rather than be bullish just yet.

It's worth noting that Evercore serves a diverse set of clients including those needing research, trading, and investment banking work, but also the individual/family or foundation needing wealth and investment management solutions.

Some forecasts say there will be a supply/demand imbalance, which I think could favor the wealth advisory niche. In January, for example, McKinsey wrote that "the advisor population itself is aging: nearly 40% of advisors are expected to retire within a decade, creating a shortfall of roughly 100,000 professionals," while also pointing out that there could be a huge market potential in the next decade to manage family wealth, saying "$14T of assets are expected to be inherited by Gen X and $8T by millennials."

The bigger question, however, is where equity markets may be heading this year, since rising market values can drive higher fees on managed assets, but also spur greater investor confidence and trading volume driven by bulls. That, of course, would be great news for Evercore.

However, as of this writing in late March, there is still uncertainty over oil shocks and geopolitical outcomes in the Mideast. Financial firm Raymond James (RJF) had a relatively positive but cautious tone in an article a few days ago, saying "oil remains the key swing factor for Fed policy, the economy and equity markets. While prices are still near $100/barrel, our base case view is that oil falls toward $60/barrel by year‑end."

Interestingly, and this shows how volatile and ever-changing this current environment is, as of Monday morning major media including BBC reported that "oil prices plunged and stock markets rebounded after Donald Trump said the US and Iran had held talks over halting the conflict in the Middle East."

So, because we are not quite out of the woods yet, and any forecast this week may change in the next two, I think on a macro level I prefer to remain in a neutral/holding pattern when it comes to this sector.

Competitive Positioning

To summarize this section, I was highly bullish on this stock when it comes to competitive positioning, due to not only better historic metrics than key peers but also momentum and a diversified business model.

The peer comparables I picked for Evercore are in the market cap range of $3B- 12B, and are within the scope of investment bank/brokerage: Lazard (LAZ), Stifel (SF), and Jefferies (JEF).

Notably from peer data and why Evercore sticks out to me is that it is the largest of this group by market cap, but also led the group with a 5-year revenue CAGR of +10.9%, and also impressed in return on equity with an ROE of +30%, the highest in this group.

Those factors above such as size, top-line growth history, and ROE history, already put Evercore into a more favorable performance position.

To also consider market momentum for Evercore stock, Evercore really got my attention with its 10-year price return, not only beating a major benchmark like the S&P500 but also the best in this peer group, showing there is lots of investor interest in this stock despite various market shocks in recent years:

That resilience and leadership position among peers certainly moves the needle into the highly bullish column, and complementing that is what I mentioned earlier and that is a diversified business model across several niches (wealth, IB, research) but also no major exposure to consumer banking or significant exposure to private credit risk.

Company Growth Factors

Next, I am compelled to be bullish on company growth factors and here is why.

From the earnings results in early February, we can see that Evercore closed out FY25 with convincing top-line results, growing net revenue YoY.

While that is historic by now, the results also pointed to something that could impact future upside and that is fees from asset management, driven by client money inflows rather than just rising equity market values. For example, Q4 AUM/A fees grew 10% YoY driven by "wealth management clients, as associated AUM increased 12%, from market appreciation and net inflows." (results, pg. 6).

Growth can come from business acquisition too, so the acquisition last year of Robey Warshaw, which Seeking Alpha called "a highly successful independent advisory firm headquartered in the United Kingdom," should reflect in this year's results, assuming integration risk is minimal.

We can also see there are growth levers in the advisory segment, with Q4 results (pg 1) mentioning not only that the firm worked on at least 15 notable transactions in FY25 but also is working on 3 major ones in FY26 already, which I think can drive fees momentum in the near-term.

After all, it seems like this is primarily a fees-driven firm, so much less prone to interest-rate risk and margin squeezes that some other financial firms, so the challenge will be to continue growing those fees organically in the near term given the macro environment going into late March.

Complementing the above is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth of nearly +21%, easily beating the sector average, and indicating there is confidence the fee-driven momentum could continue.

Profitability Metrics

When it comes to this firm's earnings potential looking ahead, I have to be bullish here again.

An indicator of core earnings efficiency in this type of business is the operating margin, since this firm's focus is unlike that of a REIT that holds a huge property portfolio and uses considerable depreciation.

We can see that Evercore had an operating margin of 21.2%, 2nd in this peer group and also on an improvement trend if you follow its line (dark blue):

Beyond that, in considering what kind of costs could impact earnings, the company mentioned in Q4 results that the Robey Warshaw acquisition had some impact, but also notable was the 33% YoY growth in non-compensation costs "driven by an increase in technology and information services," which I think points to this firm's considerable dependency on reliable tech and data, and I see that more of an investment in innovation/optimization rather than just another expense.

The consensus is also confident, giving Evercore +8 upward earnings revisions and forecasting 25 -26% YoY EPS growth this year and next, which I think should further attract bullish investors to this stock, supporting the share price. Considering the strong operating margin, and fee-driven momentum so far, it is not an unrealistic view to be honest.

Balance Sheet Risk

After considering a few points related to balance sheet risk, I am bullish on this stock, and this is driven by the D/E metrics and asset risk exposure.

As I did not find any recent credit ratings from major agencies like Fitch, Moody's, S&P Global, what I want to instead focus on is the debt-to-equity vs peers:

With a D/E of just 0.56, Evercore is near the lowest of this peer group when it comes to leverage exposure in relation to equity, and this certainly makes the risk profile more attractive to me.

Additionally, Q4 results did not mention any asset risk exposure to client loans, alternatives/private credit, or real estate portfolios. What they did say was:

The Company continues to maintain a strong balance sheet. As of December 31, 2025, cash and cash equivalents were $1.4B, investment securities and certificates of deposit were $1.6B and current assets exceeded current liabilities by $2.1B.

Of course, I mention this in the wake of recent jitters about private-credit exposure at firms like Blackrock (BLK) and others, with Morningstar writing this month that "the industry is staring down a reversal of fortune and gearing up for a high-stakes stress test of its ability to stave off a widening liquidity crunch."

This is why it is important not to lump all financial stocks into the same bucket, because it does not adequately consider their differences in asset risk.

Dividend Outlook

After considering the dividend yield, growth, payout ratio, and likelihood of further dividend coverage, I am definitely bullish and would add this stock to my dividend ideas for the month.

EVR is not exactly in my high-yield bucket, with a yield of just 1.22% and below its sector average. If yield alone is your thing, there are several REITs I've covered lately with above-5% yields.

However, where Evercore wins for me is a proven long-term dividend growth that shows it can be resilient through many cycles and market shocks, with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +11%, higher than the sector average.

Further, with a payout ratio of just 23%, it also implies a higher level of dividend safety and cash wiggle room, and if those EPS growth forecasts mentioned earlier come true then that could support further dividend growth. After all, this is not a business known for massive capex investment such as those than run manufacturing plants or grow a portfolio of real estate, or perhaps R&D spending in pharma, and in fact the quarter ending Dec. 2025 saw just under $9MM in capital expenditure.

Valuation Metrics

To summarize this section, using Seeking Alpha data as inputs, I created the following worksheet which compares valuations and also forecasts a future share price, and in this case, I am highly bullish on this stock again.

While the stock may seem very overvalued vs these peers, I can see why the market is willing to pay a premium for Evercore and earlier I highlighted its strong operating margin, robust pipeline of advisory work and fee-driven momentum, as well as strong analyst consensus forecasts.

My share price forecast for Dec. 2027 implies a +32% upside potential, and this is very close to the avg. Wall St rating as of Monday afternoon, forecasting a +35% upside, so both mine and Wall Street's forecast is very confident.

Interestingly, the consensus doesn't seem too phased about the potential risk impact from the current geopolitical and oil price shock, perhaps because Evercore has already shown it can weather various market cycles and shocks, is perhaps less exposed directly to oil-price impacts, and hardly any direct risk from interest rates such as on the level of banks and mortgage REITs with huge loan portfolios.

Technical Charting

When it comes to technical patterns, I pulled back to a neutral/hold score in this section, after considering the following chart tracking the share price over the last year, vs the 50-day and 200-day SMA:

We can clearly see a buy signal (golden cross formation) when the 50-day SMA crossed above the 200-day last summer, and there was a bullish pattern for quite a while. However, more recently, the support seems to have broken and the share price dove below the 200-day SMA, which essentially hurts the bullish view. The 50-day is also pointing downward, but a sell signal (death cross) has not quite formed yet.

This bearish pattern also challenges the overwhelmingly bullish view supported by my fundamental analysis in prior sections; however, I think it has not lasted that long, and the long-term indicator (200-day SMA) is still trending upward.

Some investors may view this as a dip-buying opportunity, given the otherwise strong fundamentals. However, that assumes general market malaise (from geopolitical and oil shocks) will recover soon, which is hard to gauge.

So, I am neutral here.

Risks and Challenge to Thesis

A key element supporting my bullish thesis could also challenge it, and that is Evercore being a robust and diverse fee-driven firm, which means that if there are negative impacts to fees (lower deal flows, market declines, lower client inflows, lower demand for equities research) it is a considerable hit to revenue and earnings, and there is no significant interest income to fall back on such as with major banks who may be diversified across both fees and interest income, but also market making and trading their own capital.

So any bullish thesis assumes the fees-focused business model will continue to execute and grow optimally in many different market cycles and macro conditions, and this could be a risk factor to consider.

Conclusion

To sum up what we covered today, I showed why I agree with Wall Street's buy rating and how upside can come from Evercore's investment banking and wealth shops, but also that it has already proven itself as a great dividend income idea, a low balance sheet risk idea, and a strong margin idea, and so I recommend considering Evercore in your watchlist of potential buys for the long-term investment portfolio.
